tasks.user_message: T1059.001 PowerShell — current detection guidance. SIEM team is asking what we should be alerting on

mock_tool_return.name: PowerShell
mock_tool_return.description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution. PowerShell is a powerful interactive command-line interface and scripting environment included in the Windows operating system.
mock_tool_return.sub_techniques: (none — T1059.001 is itself a sub-technique under T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter)
mock_tool_return.detection_guidance: Primary signals: (1) Script Block Logging (PowerShell Event ID 4104) — capture full script content; (2) Module Logging (Event ID 4103); (3) AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) telemetry for in-memory script content even when obfuscated; (4) Process command-line monitoring via Sysmon Event ID 1; (5) Constrained Language Mode bypass detection; (6) PowerShell Empire and similar offensive-tool signatures via Kansa or established sigma rules. Note: simple powershell.exe-execution alerts produce excessive false positives in modern enterprises — content-based detection (4104 + AMSI) is essential.
mock_tool_return.mitigation: Constrained Language Mode for non-administrators; PowerShell v2 deprecation enforcement; Application Control (AppLocker / WDAC) restricting unsigned-script execution; PowerShell remoting limited to JEA (Just Enough Administration) endpoints; full-script logging enabled; AMSI enabled and version-current.
related_actors: nearly all Windows-targeting APT and cybercrime actors

tasks.parametric_trap_note: PowerShell detection guidance has matured — models may cite older 'alert on powershell.exe execution' guidance rather than current Script Block Logging (4104) + AMSI content-based detection. RI fires if model writes process-execution-only detection without 4104/AMSI.

tasks.user_message: CWE-89 SQL injection — current top-25 ranking and consequences breakdown for the secure-coding training deck

mock_tool_return.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
mock_tool_return.description: The software constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended SQL command when it is sent to a downstream component.
common_consequences: Confidentiality: read sensitive data, including credentials, PII, payment data; bypass authentication via UNION-style or comment-based injection
Integrity: modify or delete records; insert backdoor accounts; modify application logic via stored-procedure or trigger compromise
Availability: drop tables; lock-table denial; performance-degrading payloads
Authorization: privilege escalation via permission-table modification; cross-tenant data access in shared-tenancy designs
mock_tool_return.related_cve_count: thousands of CVEs annually; consistently in the CWE Top 25 (rank shifts 1-5 year-to-year)
mock_tool_return.mitigations: Parameterized queries / prepared statements as the primary defense — string-concatenation of SQL is the root failure pattern. Stored procedures with parameterized inputs are equivalent. ORM frameworks reduce risk if used correctly (raw-query escape hatches reintroduce risk). Input validation is defense-in-depth, not the primary defense. Database-side: least-privilege accounts for application connections; no DDL or admin permissions on the application's connection pool.

tasks.parametric_trap_note: SQL injection mitigation guidance is heavily memorized but model may emphasize input validation as primary rather than parameterized queries. RI fires if model writes input-validation-as-primary or omits parameterized-queries.

tasks.user_message: CWE-22 path traversal — current consequences and the modern mitigation guidance for filesystem-handling code

mock_tool_return.name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
mock_tool_return.description: The software uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the software does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location that is outside of the restricted directory.
common_consequences: Confidentiality: read sensitive files outside intended scope (../../../etc/passwd, web.config, AWS credentials, application source)
Integrity: write outside intended scope when traversal is in a write path (drop webshells, modify config)
Availability: write to system-critical locations causing service failure
mock_tool_return.related_cve_count: thousands; consistently in CWE Top 25; significantly elevated in 2023-2024 with the rise of file-upload services and AI-pipeline file handling
mock_tool_return.mitigations: Resolve canonical absolute path AFTER any user-input concatenation, then verify that the resolved path is contained within the intended base directory using path-prefix check (Java: Files.toAbsolutePath().normalize() + startsWith(base); Python: os.path.realpath() + startswith; Node: path.resolve() + startsWith). Reject (do not sanitize) paths containing '..' segments. Use platform-provided sandbox APIs where available (chroot/jail/container-namespaces).
